initial_diagnostic_question.question: Does this ECG suggest the presence of anterior myocardial infarction?

criterion_selection.question: To accurately diagnose anterior myocardial infarction, which of the following diagnostic criteria should be evaluated?
options: Presence of diffuse concave ST-segment elevation across precordial leads
Presence of spiked pacemaker artifacts preceding the QRS complex
Presence of ST-segment depression in leads V2, V3, and V4
Presence of ST-segment elevation in anterior leads
criterion_selection.answer: Presence of ST-segment elevation in anterior leads

finding.question: Regarding the criterion you selected, does this ECG show ST elevation in at least two of the anterior leads, including leads V1, V2, V3, and V4?

grounding.question: To verify that the anterior leads do not show ST elevation, in which of the following segments can you locate the QRS complex in these leads? The options below refer to time ranges on the ECG signal, provided in seconds.

decision.question: Based on the finding identified above, does this patient have anterior myocardial infarction?
options: Further findings are required to confirm the diagnosis
decision.answer: Further findings are required to confirm the diagnosis

criterion_selection.question: In addition to the finding you just identified, which other diagnostic criterion should be evaluated to diagnose anterior myocardial infarction?
options: Presence of a Delta wave (slurred upstroke) in anterior leads
Presence of pathological Q waves in anterior leads
Presence of low voltage QRS complexes in precordial leads
Presence of an RSR' pattern in lead V1
criterion_selection.answer: Presence of pathological Q waves in anterior leads

finding.question: Regarding the criterion you selected, does this ECG show pathological Q waves in at least two of the anterior leads, including leads V1, V2, V3, and V4? Note that a pathological Q wave in these leads is defined as (1) a Q wave with a duration greater than 30 milliseconds and an amplitude greater than 0.075mV, or (2) a deep S wave with only a small initial R wave preceding it.

grounding.question: To verify that the anterior leads do not show pathological Q waves, in which of the following segments can you locate the QRS complex in these leads? The options below refer to time ranges on the ECG signal, provided in seconds.

decision.question: Based on all the findings identified so far, does this patient have anterior myocardial infarction?
options: Further findings are required to confirm the diagnosis
